response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend a lot of time scrolling through social media during my delivery breaks and after work, and honestly the whole vaccine discussion has become absolutely exhausting. Everyone acts like they're some kind of expert after reading three posts on Facebook or watching a YouTube video. When people start arguing about COVID vaccines around me, I usually just tune out because it always becomes this heated mess where nobody actually listens to anyone else. Working as a delivery driver in Bologna during the pandemic, I saw everything from both sides. I delivered to hospitals, to people who were terrified, to people who thought the whole thing was overblown. What I learned is that most people have already made up their minds and no amount of discussion will change that. I'm not going to pretend I understand all the science behind vaccines because I don't have that kind of education, but I also think people should stop acting like they do when they clearly don't either. My approach is pretty straightforward: I avoid these conversations when I can because they stress me out and go nowhere productive. When I can't avoid them, like when family brings it up, I just say I'm not interested in debating it. People get upset with me for not engaging, but I don't care. I've got a kid to think about and a job to do, and spending hours arguing online or at dinner tables about vaccines doesn't pay my bills or make anyone happier. If someone asks me directly what I think, I tell them to talk to their actual doctor, not their cousin who shared some conspiracy theory. The problem with these discussions is everyone wants to be right more than they want to actually understand anything. Social media has made it worse because algorithms just show people what confirms what they already believe. I see it constantly. My feed shows me gaming content and nature videos because that's what I interact with, but I know others are getting fed completely different realities. At the end of the day, healthcare decisions should be between people and their doctors, not debated endlessly by random people online who think they've done research.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 27-year-old delivery driver with primary school education, married with one child, living in Bologna. Key traits influencing the response: low agreeableness (15) suggests blunt, direct communication and lack of interest in pleasing others; high openness (91) allows for considering different perspectives but combined with low conscientiousness (31) means not deeply researching topics; moderate neuroticism (61) indicates some anxiety about stressful topics; moderate extraversion (40) suggests selective social engagement. Heavy social media use provides exposure to debates but also fatigue. Working-class occupation during pandemic gives practical perspective. Agnostic beliefs suggest non-dogmatic approach. Low agreeableness particularly important for dismissive, somewhat confrontational tone about others' opinions. Creative trait might manifest in unique problem-solving approach of simply avoiding unproductive discussions.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, somewhat dismissive, practical, mildly confrontational, stressed, straightforward, working-class perspective
citations: Personal experience as delivery driver during COVID-19 pandemic
response_parsed.safety_notes: Response acknowledges scientific expertise exists with medical professionals while expressing personal frustration with online debates. Does not promote misinformation but reflects realistic frustration with polarized discussions. Appropriately suggests consulting doctors for medical decisions.
